title: Capturing Structural Locality in Non-parametric Language Models

structured.summary: This paper proposes incorporating structural locality features into kNN-based non-parametric language models by learning locality-conditioned linear transformations of retrieval distances. Evaluated on Wikipedia and Java source code datasets, the method improves perplexity and token prediction accuracy by biasing retrieval towards more relevant local contexts without retraining the underlying parametric model.

strengths.point: Novel incorporation of structural locality into kNN retrieval
strengths.evidence: The paper formalizes hierarchical locality levels (e.g., same article, same section) and integrates them via learned linear distance transformations, improving retrieval relevance.
Experiments on both natural language (Wikipedia) and programming language (Java) datasets show consistent perplexity and accuracy gains over baseline kNN-LMs.
The approach requires learning only a small number of parameters (w_n, b_n) per locality level and does not require retraining the parametric LM.

strengths.point: Detailed analysis of locality feature impact
strengths.evidence: The paper analyzes how locality features complement standard distance metrics and provides qualitative examples illustrating improved retrieval quality.

weaknesses.point: Lack of comparison to prior learned distance metric methods
weaknesses.evidence: No baselines include metric learning approaches for kNN retrieval without explicit locality features, making it unclear if gains stem from locality or general distance adaptation.

weaknesses.point: Insufficient justification and exploration of linear distance transformation
weaknesses.evidence: The choice of a simple linear form for g_n(d) is not motivated or ablated against more complex transformations.

weaknesses.point: Assumption of mutually exclusive locality levels without handling overlaps
weaknesses.evidence: The method assumes exclusive locality categories but does not discuss overlapping or nested locality attributes common in real data.

weaknesses.point: Missing baselines using structural locality without learned distance parameters
weaknesses.evidence: No experiments test simple heuristics or hard filters based on locality to isolate the benefit of learning w_n, b_n.

weaknesses.point: Limited evaluation metrics and downstream task assessment
weaknesses.evidence: Evaluation focuses on perplexity and token accuracy without standard IR metrics or downstream task performance to assess practical impact.

weaknesses.point: No ablation on number or granularity of locality levels
weaknesses.evidence: The paper fixes 5 or 7 locality levels but does not analyze sensitivity to this design choice.
Validation set construction and parameter learning hyperparameters are not specified.

weaknesses.point: Ambiguity in locality level definitions and test-time integration
weaknesses.evidence: Definitions of Wikipedia locality categories and the procedure for test-time adaptation lack clarity.

weaknesses.point: No discussion of scalability or overfitting risks
weaknesses.evidence: The paper does not address how the method scales to very large datastores or potential overfitting to sparse locality annotations.
Only Wikipedia and Java datasets are evaluated; no experiments on other domains or downstream tasks.
Improvements in perplexity and accuracy lack confidence intervals or significance analysis.

questions.question: How does the proposed method compare to existing metric learning approaches for kNN retrieval without explicit locality features?
questions.motivation: Understanding this would clarify whether gains arise from structural locality or simply from learning distance adaptations.

questions.question: Can the method handle overlapping or nested locality attributes common in real-world datasets?
questions.motivation: This affects the applicability and robustness of the approach beyond mutually exclusive locality levels.

questions.question: How sensitive is performance to the choice and granularity of locality levels?
questions.motivation: Ablation on locality hierarchy design would inform best practices and generalizability.

questions.question: What are the scalability implications when applying multiple locality-conditioned distance computations on very large datastores?
questions.motivation: Practical deployment requires understanding computational overhead and efficiency.

questions.question: Does improved retrieval with locality features translate to better downstream task performance or generation quality?
questions.motivation: This would demonstrate practical impact beyond language modeling metrics.

critique_points.point_001: Lack of comparison to prior learned distance metric methods. No baselines include metric learning approaches for kNN retrieval without explicit locality features, making it unclear if gains stem from locality or general distance adaptation.
critique_points.point_002: Insufficient justification and exploration of linear distance transformation. The choice of a simple linear form for g_n(d) is not motivated or ablated against more complex transformations.
critique_points.point_003: Assumption of mutually exclusive locality levels without handling overlaps. The method assumes exclusive locality categories but does not discuss overlapping or nested locality attributes common in real data.
critique_points.point_004: Missing baselines using structural locality without learned distance parameters. No experiments test simple heuristics or hard filters based on locality to isolate the benefit of learning w_n, b_n.
critique_points.point_005: Limited evaluation metrics and downstream task assessment. Evaluation focuses on perplexity and token accuracy without standard IR metrics or downstream task performance to assess practical impact.
critique_points.point_006: No ablation on number or granularity of locality levels. The paper fixes 5 or 7 locality levels but does not analyze sensitivity to this design choice.
critique_points.point_007: Reproducibility details incomplete. Validation set construction and parameter learning hyperparameters are not specified.
critique_points.point_008: Ambiguity in locality level definitions and test-time integration. Definitions of Wikipedia locality categories and the procedure for test-time adaptation lack clarity.
critique_points.point_009: No discussion of scalability or overfitting risks. The paper does not address how the method scales to very large datastores or potential overfitting to sparse locality annotations.
critique_points.point_010: Limited generalizability experiments. Only Wikipedia and Java datasets are evaluated; no experiments on other domains or downstream tasks.
critique_points.point_011: No statistical significance testing reported. Improvements in perplexity and accuracy lack confidence intervals or significance analysis.

transcript.reader:
**Structured Summary**

---

### Problem & Motivation

- **Problem:** Non-parametric language models (LMs), which augment parametric LMs by retrieving examples from external datastores, rely heavily on distance metrics (e.g., L2 distance between context vectors) to retrieve relevant contexts. However, these metrics alone do not fully capture the semantic or structural relationships between contexts, limiting retrieval quality and thus LM performance.

- **Motivation:** Real-world datasets often exhibit **structural locality**, where data points are organized into hierarchical local groups sharing attributes (e.g., topical clusters in Wikipedia articles, project/package hierarchies in source code). Incorporating such structural locality into non-parametric LMs could improve retrieval relevance and language modeling performance.

---

### Proposed Method

- **Key Idea:** Introduce **structural locality features** as categorical, hierarchical attributes that indicate whether two contexts share common properties (e.g., same Wikipedia article category or section, same project or subdirectory in source code).

- **Implementation:**
  - Define multiple mutually exclusive locality levels (e.g., for Wikipedia: no locality, same article, same section title, same article category; for Java source code: no locality, same project, same subdirectory).
  - Modify the kNN retrieval distance metric by learning a **linear transformation** of the original distance conditioned on locality features:
    \[
    g_n(d) = w_n \cdot d + b_n
    \]
    where \(w_n, b_n\) are learned parameters for each locality level \(l_n\).
  - Use these modified distances to compute the kNN probability distribution, effectively biasing retrieval towards more local contexts.
  - Learn the parameters \(w_n, b_n\) by minimizing negative log-likelihood on a validation set, requiring only a small number of parameters (5 for Java, 7 for Wikipedia).

- **Advantages:**
  - Simple, lightweight, and easily integrated into existing non-parametric LMs.
  - Does not require retraining the underlying parametric LM.
  - Supports multiple hierarchical locality levels.
  - Enables test-time adaptation without retraining.

---

### Results

- **Datasets:**
  - **Wikipedia (WikiText-103):** Natural language text with ~100M tokens training data.
  - **Java GitHub Corpus:** Source code from 13,362 Java projects (~1.44B tokens training data).

- **Base Models:**
  - Transformer-based parametric LM.
  - kNN-LM augmenting the Transformer with nearest neighbor retrieval.

- **Findings:**
  - Adding kNN retrieval improves perplexity and token prediction accuracy significantly over parametric LM alone in both domains.
  - Incorporating structural locality features further improves performance:
    - Wikipedia: ~16% perplexity reduction with kNN; additional consistent gains with locality.
    - Java source code: ~29% perplexity reduction with kNN; further ~4.9% relative error reduction with locality features.
  - Locality features especially improve top-ranked neighbor discrimination and token prediction accuracy.
  - Analysis shows that:
    - In Wikipedia, locality features provide complementary information beyond context vector distances, especially for same article category and section title.
    - In Java code, distance metrics already correlate well with locality, but locality features still improve retrieval quality, particularly for neighbors in the same subdirectory.
  - Qualitative examples demonstrate that locality features help prioritize more relevant retrieved contexts, improving next-token probability estimates.

---

### Claimed Contributions

1. **Novel Concept:** Identification and formalization of **structural locality** as a useful property for non-parametric language modeling.

2. **Method:** A simple, effective approach to incorporate hierarchical locality features into kNN-based non-parametric LMs by learning locality-conditioned distance transformations.

3. **Empirical Validation:** Demonstration of the method’s effectiveness on two diverse domains (natural language and programming language), showing consistent improvements in perplexity and token prediction accuracy.

4. **Analysis:** Detailed investigation of how locality features complement traditional context similarity metrics and why they are necessary for capturing hierarchical data structure.

5. **Practical Implications:** The approach enables better retrieval-based language modeling without retraining, supports multiple locality levels, and can be applied to various domains exhibiting structural locality.

6. **Broader Impact Discussion:** Consideration of ethical issues related to improved language models, including potential misuse, copyright, privacy, and environmental impact.

---

### Additional Notes

- The method requires only a small number of additional parameters and a small annotated validation set for parameter tuning.

- The approach is domain-agnostic and can be extended to other datasets with hierarchical or structural locality (e.g., books, research papers, product manuals).

- The authors provide reproducibility details and release code and data processing scripts.

---

**In summary, this paper proposes and validates a lightweight method to enhance non-parametric language models by explicitly incorporating structural locality information, resulting in improved retrieval quality and language modeling performance across natural and programming language domains.**
transcript.critic:
1. **Novelty - Missing Comparison to Learned Distance Metrics:**  
The paper proposes learning a linear transformation of the kNN distance conditioned on locality features (Section "Proposed Method"). However, it does not compare against prior work that learns or adapts distance metrics for kNN retrieval without explicit locality features (e.g., metric learning approaches in retrieval or kNN-LMs). Including such baselines would clarify whether the gains come specifically from incorporating structural locality or from any learned distance adaptation.

2. **Methodology - Justification for Linear Distance Transformation:**  
The choice of a simple linear transformation \(g_n(d) = w_n \cdot d + b_n\) per locality level is not sufficiently justified (Section "Proposed Method"). The paper does not explore or motivate why a linear form is adequate or optimal, nor does it ablate more complex transformations (e.g., nonlinear or learned embeddings). This limits understanding of whether the simplicity sacrifices potential performance.

3. **Methodology - Assumption of Mutually Exclusive Locality Levels:**  
The method assumes mutually exclusive locality levels (e.g., same article vs. same section) (Section "Proposed Method"), but real-world data may have overlapping or nested locality attributes. The paper does not discuss how overlapping locality features are handled or whether the method generalizes to such cases, which could limit applicability.

4. **Evaluation - Lack of Baselines Using Structural Features Without Distance Learning:**  
The paper does not include a baseline where structural locality features are used as hard filters or simple re-ranking heuristics without learning \(w_n, b_n\) parameters (Section "Results"). This would help isolate the benefit of learned distance transformations versus naive use of locality information.

5. **Evaluation - Limited Metrics Focused on Perplexity and Accuracy:**  
While perplexity and token prediction accuracy are reported (Section "Results"), the paper does not evaluate retrieval quality with standard information retrieval metrics (e.g., precision@k, recall@k) or downstream task performance. This limits understanding of how locality features improve retrieval beyond language modeling metrics.

6. **Evaluation - No Ablation on Number or Granularity of Locality Levels:**  
The paper defines 5 locality levels for Java and 7 for Wikipedia (Section "Proposed Method") but does not ablate the impact of including fewer or more granular locality levels. Such analysis would clarify the sensitivity of the method to the chosen hierarchy design.

7. **Reproducibility - Missing Details on Validation Set Construction:**  
The paper states that parameters \(w_n, b_n\) are learned by minimizing negative log-likelihood on a validation set (Section "Proposed Method") but does not specify how this validation set is constructed, its size, or whether it is disjoint from training and test sets. This omission hinders reproducibility.

8. **Reproducibility - Lack of Hyperparameter and Training Details for Parameter Learning:**  
Details such as optimization algorithm, learning rate, number of epochs, and initialization for learning \(w_n, b_n\) are not provided (Section "Proposed Method"). These are critical for reproducing the parameter tuning procedure.

9. **Clarity - Ambiguity in Definition of Locality Levels for Wikipedia:**  
The paper lists locality levels such as "same article category" and "same section title" (Section "Proposed Method") but does not clearly define how these categories or sections are extracted or standardized, which may confuse readers unfamiliar with Wikipedia’s structure.

10. **Limitations - No Discussion of Scalability to Very Large Datastores:**  
Although the method is lightweight, the paper does not discuss how the additional locality-conditioned distance computations scale when the datastore grows very large, especially with multiple locality levels (Section "Advantages"). This is important for practical deployment.

11. **Limitations - Potential Overfitting to Locality Features Not Addressed:**  
The paper does not analyze whether learning separate parameters \(w_n, b_n\) per locality level risks overfitting to the validation set, particularly given the small number of parameters but potentially sparse locality annotations (Section "Proposed Method").

12. **Generalisability - No Experiments Beyond Wikipedia and Java Source Code:**  
While the paper claims domain-agnostic applicability (Section "Additional Notes"), experiments are limited to only two datasets. It remains unclear how well the method generalizes to other domains with different or less well-defined locality structures (e.g., conversational data, scientific papers).

13. **Generalisability - No Evaluation on Downstream Tasks or Generation Quality:**  
The evaluation focuses on perplexity and token accuracy but does not assess whether improved retrieval with locality features translates to better downstream task performance (e.g., question answering) or generation quality, limiting claims about practical impact.

14. **Clarity - Insufficient Explanation of How Locality Features Are Integrated at Test Time:**  
The paper mentions test-time adaptation without retraining (Section "Advantages") but does not clearly explain the procedure or whether locality parameters can be updated online or require batch tuning, which may confuse practitioners.

15. **Evaluation - No Statistical Significance Testing Reported:**  
Reported improvements in perplexity and accuracy (Section "Results") lack statistical significance analysis or confidence intervals, making it difficult to assess the robustness of the gains attributed to locality features.
